skill.name: bash-linux
skill.description: Guides Bash and Linux terminal work with command patterns, file operations, process management, networking, scripting, and error handling.
skill.summary: Use practical Bash and Linux terminal patterns for shell work on macOS and Linux.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution patterns, but review shows they are Markdown examples in SKILL.md rather than hidden executable code. The skill is still medium risk because it intentionally teaches shell, process, network, and filesystem commands that an agent could run with local system impact.

medium_findings.title: Broad Shell Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill documents command chaining, file inspection, text processing, environment variables, script templates, and error handling for Bash. This is legitimate for a Bash reference, but the supported tool context includes shell access, so careless use can run commands on the local system.
medium_findings.reasoning: The static external command findings are not Ruby execution or hidden malware; they are Markdown command examples. They remain relevant because the skill is explicitly designed to guide terminal command execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The affected lines are visible Markdown examples, and the skill metadata allows Bash. That confirms operational command risk without evidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Process Termination Examples
medium_findings.description: The process management section includes force-kill patterns for PIDs and ports. These commands can interrupt user workloads or services if adapted without checking the target process.
medium_findings.reasoning: The commands are common administrative examples, but forceful termination is inherently destructive when run against the wrong process.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly show kill commands. The risk is contextual rather than malicious because the surrounding section is a process management reference.

medium_findings.title: Network Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The network section includes download, API request, POST, port check, and network interface commands. These can contact external services or reveal local network details when used with real targets.
medium_findings.reasoning: The hardcoded URLs are example.com placeholders and not evidence of exfiltration. The capability is still sensitive because the skill teaches outbound requests and network discovery commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network commands are explicit and grouped under a network heading. I found no hidden endpoint or data exfiltration instruction.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: Most Ruby backtick, command substitution, Unix shell invocation, and PowerShell invocation findings are caused by Markdown inline code and fenced Bash examples. They are documentation examples, not code executed by the skill file itself.
low_findings.reasoning: SKILL.md contains reference tables and fenced examples. I found no embedded runner, installer, obfuscated payload, prompt injection, or instruction to skip security review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a Markdown-only skill document, and the flagged syntax appears in documented examples. No executable source files or hidden scripts were present in the analyzed file list.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Finding Is Not Supported
low_findings.description: The static weak cryptographic algorithm finding points to the front matter description, which uses the word critical. No cryptographic algorithm or crypto operation is present at that line.
low_findings.reasoning: Line 3 is plain metadata describing critical commands. No evidence found for weak cryptography in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is visible front matter text and contains no cryptographic primitive. This is a clear scanner mismatch.

low_findings.title: Temporary File Cleanup Example
low_findings.description: The cleanup example removes a named file under /tmp. This is a common pattern, but users should adapt it carefully to avoid deleting the wrong path.
low_findings.reasoning: The filesystem finding is a real file deletion example, but it targets a fixed temporary filename in documentation and does not run automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line explicitly shows a file removal command. The risk is limited because it is documentation and the target is a temporary example path.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Execution Patterns
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains examples for command chaining, pipes, process control, networking, and script execution. These patterns can change local state if an agent runs adapted commands.
dangerous_patterns.reasoning: The examples are expected for a Bash skill, but they require user confirmation and careful target validation before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and numerous. I found no automatic execution path inside the Markdown file.

content.user_title: Run Bash and Linux Commands Safely
content.value_statement: Terminal work can fail when commands are copied without context or error handling. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Bash patterns for safer Linux and macOS workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains Bash command chaining with sequential, success, failure, and pipe operators.
Provides common file inspection, search, disk usage, and text processing patterns.
Shows process management patterns, including port lookup, background jobs, and foreground jobs.
Documents environment variable usage for viewing, setting, and extending PATH values.
Includes a Bash script template with strict mode, logging functions, and cleanup traps.
Compares common PowerShell commands with equivalent Bash commands.
limitations: It is a reference skill, not a full Bash course or security hardening guide.
It does not validate command targets before an agent runs them.
It does not include platform-specific package manager instructions.
It assumes the user is working on Linux or macOS with standard shell tools.

use_cases.title: Debug a Local Development Environment
use_cases.description: Use process, port, file, and log commands to inspect a local service and identify common runtime issues.

use_cases.title: Write Safer Bash Automation
use_cases.description: Use strict mode, cleanup traps, command existence checks, and clear logging when building shell scripts.

use_cases.title: Translate Terminal Habits from PowerShell
use_cases.description: Map familiar PowerShell operations to Bash equivalents for files, environment variables, strings, and pipelines.

prompt_templates.title: Find the Right Command
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to inspect files in a Linux project. Suggest the safest Bash commands to list files, view file sizes, and search for text.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose a Port Conflict
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me investigate what is using port 3000 on macOS or Linux. Explain each command before suggesting any process termination.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Bash Script Skeleton
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft a Bash script outline that uses strict error handling, clear logging, a main function, and a cleanup trap.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Shell Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Bash workflow for quoting, error handling, destructive operations, and network calls. Recommend safer alternatives where needed.

output_examples.input: User asks how to inspect a large project directory.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends listing hidden files, checking disk usage, previewing file contents, and searching within matching file types.

output_examples.input: User asks why a development server cannot bind to a port.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains how to identify the process using the port, verify it is safe to stop, and then choose a careful next action.

output_examples.input: User asks for a resilient Bash script pattern.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes strict mode, clear logging, a main entry point, command existence checks, and cleanup on exit.

best_practices: Ask the assistant to explain commands before running anything that changes files, processes, or network state.
Use strict mode, quoted variables, and explicit error handling when writing shell scripts.
Replace placeholder paths, ports, and URLs with verified values before execution.
anti_patterns: Do not run force-kill commands until you confirm the target process is correct.
Do not paste network commands with real tokens, secrets, or private endpoints into shared prompts.
Do not adapt cleanup examples to broad paths or wildcard deletions without review.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a Markdown reference. An AI tool may still suggest commands based on it, so review commands before running them.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for beginners?
faq.answer: It can help beginners, but shell commands can affect local files and processes. Ask for explanations before execution.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill teaches real shell, network, filesystem, and process operations. These are useful but can cause damage if used incorrectly.

faq.question: Does it include malicious network endpoints?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The cited URLs use example.com placeholders and standard local network examples.

faq.question: Can it help with Bash scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a script template with strict mode, logging, a main function, and cleanup handling.

faq.question: Does it work on Windows PowerShell?
faq.answer: It is focused on Bash for Linux and macOS. It includes a small comparison table for PowerShell users.
